title: Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Tree

content.problemStatement:
Given a binary tree and two node values n1n1n1 and n2n2n2, find their lowest common ancestor (LCA). The LCA is defined as the deepest node in the tree that is an ancestor of both nodes.


Input Format

First line contains TTT, the number of test cases
For each test case:

First line contains NNN, the number of nodes in the binary tree
Second line contains NNN space-separated integers representing the level-order traversal of the tree (null nodes represented by -1)
Third line contains two integers n1n1n1 and n2n2n2




Output Format

For each test case, print the value of the lowest common ancestor node.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains N, the number of entries in the level‑order traversal (including -1 for null nodes). Second line contains N space‑separated integers representing the tree in level order, where -1 denotes a missing node. Third line contains two integers n1 and n2, the values of the two nodes whose LCA is to be found.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the value of the lowest common ancestor of n1 and n2. If either node is absent, print -1.

solution.approach: The tree is given in level order with -1 placeholders, so we first reconstruct the binary tree using a queue. While building, we skip -1 entries. Once the tree is built, the LCA can be found with a simple recursive traversal: for each node, if it matches one of the targets we return it; otherwise we recurse on left and right sub‑trees. If both sub‑calls return non‑null, the current node is the LCA; if only one returns non‑null, propagate that upward. This runs in O(N) time per test case and uses O(H) recursion stack where H is the height of the tree (worst‑case O(N)).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the tree plus O(H) recursion stack
